His career nearly ended after a 1995 arrest for lewd conduct in Los Angeles, but his self-deprecating 'I did a very bad thing' interview with Jay Leno is credited with saving it.

His wet-shirt emergence from a lake as Mr. Darcy in the 1995 BBC 'Pride and Prejudice' is ranked one of the greatest moments in British television history, and the image was later commemorated with a giant lake statue.

He trained as an amateur boxer in Belfast before becoming an actor, and his 'particular set of skills' speech in 'Taken', written in an afternoon, became one of the most quoted monologues of the 2000s.

He came out as gay in 1988 during a BBC radio debate on Section 28, an act of courage at the time, and has since said he refuses to act in any production for a country that criminalises homosexuality.
He was so unknown when cast as Captain Picard that the 'Star Trek: TNG' crew assumed the bald Englishman was a joke, yet he went on to redefine the franchise and earn a knighthood.

She won her Oscar for 'Shakespeare in Love' with just eight minutes of screen time as Queen Elizabeth I, one of the shortest winning performances in history, and has macular degeneration that leaves her nearly blind.
She was filming 'Downton Abbey' during chemotherapy for breast cancer, and many of the Dowager Countess's devastating one-liners were delivered between treatments.

With 21 Academy Award nominations, far more than any other actor, she is known for total dialect immersion; for 'Sophie's Choice' she learned both Polish and German, and co-stars reported she would answer only in character.
Her fee for 'Erin Brockovich', $20 million, made her the first actress to earn that sum for a single film, and she spent three months working at the real Brockovich's law firm in preparation.

After her divorce from Tom Cruise, she reportedly told friends 'at least I can wear heels now,' and went on to win the Oscar for 'The Hours' wearing a prosthetic nose.
She is one of very few actors to win Oscars in both leading and supporting categories, and co-directed Sydney Theatre Company while simultaneously shooting Hollywood blockbusters.

She sued her own studio for the right to choose her own roles, a legal battle she lost but which eventually transformed how Hollywood treated its contract players.

He was a convicted criminal at 16 and a Calvin Klein underwear model at 20 before reinventing himself as a serious dramatic actor opposite the likes of Jack Nicholson.

His rapid-fire, improvisational delivery in Swingers, a micro-budget film shot in secret at real Hollywood venues, made him a star and created the template for a decade of bro comedies.

His portrayal of a clueless elf who discovers he's human launched a Christmas movie franchise, but his most daring move was performing an entire Broadway show in character as his fictional country singer alter ego.

He was rejected by USC's film school twice, snuck onto the Universal Studios lot to observe productions, and eventually talked his way into a directing deal: before making Jaws, which invented the summer blockbuster.

He wrote the screenplays for Pulp Fiction and True Romance while working in a video rental store, drawing on an encyclopedic knowledge of cinema absorbed entirely outside of any film school.

He sold the Star Wars merchandising rights to himself rather than the studio in lieu of a higher directing fee, a decision worth billions that changed how Hollywood thought about intellectual property forever.
He was so obsessed with perfection that he filmed one scene in The Shining 127 times, and kept the specific lens he used for Barry Lyndon a closely guarded secret for years.
He was nearly fired from The Godfather multiple times during production, but the finished film went on to win three Oscars and redefine what American cinema could be.

He was fired from Disney as an animator because his drawings were considered too dark and unsettling, then returned as one of Hollywood's most commercially successful directors of dark, unsettling films.

His films are so visually distinctive, centered compositions, pastel palettes, precisely symmetrical frames, that his aesthetic has been parodied in hundreds of internet videos and even a fake AI-generated trailer.
He financed She's Gotta Have It by maxing out credit cards and calling in every favor he had in Brooklyn, then turned the film's success into a platform for unflinching commentary on race in America.

He grew up on screen as a child actor, stepped behind the camera, and won the Best Director Oscar for A Beautiful Mind: one of the few people to succeed fully on both sides of the lens.
He wrote, directed, and starred in a film almost every single year for five decades, a prolific output that also includes multiple Grammy-winning comedy albums recorded before he ever made a movie.
He spent a decade as one half of a sketch comedy duo before writing and directing Get Out, which won him the Oscar for Best Original Screenplay: the first Black writer to win that award solo.

He created a mystery-box approach to storytelling so influential, seeding unresolvable questions to keep audiences hooked, that it became both a filmmaking strategy and a cultural critique.
He kept a notebook of ideas and sketches for decades before turning them into films: the notebook itself was displayed as an art object when it was eventually published.

He has retired and un-retired multiple times, but each farewell seemed to produce another masterpiece, including The Boy and the Heron, made in his eighties, which won the Academy Award for Best Animated Feature.
She performed at the Super Bowl halftime show while secretly pregnant, and her visual album Lemonade, released as a complete surprise, redefined what a pop album release could be.
He built a business empire worth over $2.5 billion spanning music, streaming, sports management, and alcohol, becoming the first rapper to become a billionaire.

She turned a Super Bowl halftime performance into a pregnancy announcement watched by 120 million people, and her Fenty Beauty line, built on radical shade inclusivity, revolutionized the cosmetics industry.
She re-recorded her first six albums to reclaim ownership of her music after a catalogue dispute, turning a legal strategy into a pop cultural movement that sent old songs back to number one.
Her album 21 spent 24 non-consecutive weeks at number one in the United States and sold over 30 million copies worldwide, making it one of the best-selling albums of the 21st century.
She arrived at the 2010 VMAs in a dress made entirely of raw meat, but also composed and performed a Broadway-quality score for A Star Is Born, earning an Oscar for Best Original Song.
She became the first artist to have five number-one singles from a single album, Teenage Dream, matching a record previously held only by Michael Jackson.
Her whistle-register notes are among the highest ever recorded in pop, and her ponytail became so iconic that costume shops stock it as a standalone Halloween accessory.
She recorded her debut album in her brother's childhood bedroom, became the first person born in the 2000s to top the charts, and won all four major Grammy categories in a single night at age 18.
Her album Future Nostalgia was recorded before the pandemic and released into lockdown, where its disco-inflected escapism became the soundtrack to a world stuck indoors.

She wrote 'drivers license' about a heartbreak and released it as a debut single: it shattered Spotify's single-day streaming record and spent eight weeks at number one before she had a full album out.
She spent years as a Disney Channel regular before 'Espresso', a fizzy, self-penned earworm, went globally viral in 2024 and made her one of pop's biggest breakout stars.
She performed a notorious twerking routine at the VMAs that generated more tweets per minute than any previous event, then followed it with an album that revealed a genuinely powerful rock voice.

She quietly became the most-followed person on Instagram for years while managing a kidney transplant in private, and her candid discussions of mental health helped shift public conversation around celebrity wellness.

She publicly documented her struggles with eating disorders and addiction in a documentary series, turning her own story into a platform for mental health advocacy long before it was a mainstream celebrity cause.
She was cast alongside Britney Spears on The Mickey Mouse Club as a child, then out-vocalized virtually every contemporary with a five-octave range that earned her the nickname 'the Voice'.

The opening bars of '...Baby One More Time' are among the most recognizable in pop history, and her 2007 public breakdown, and the conservatorship that followed, sparked a worldwide Free Britney movement years later.

She was paid $1 million for Selena in 1997, making her the first Latina actress to earn that figure, then crossed over into music and became simultaneously one of Hollywood's biggest stars and pop's most durable hitmakers.

She owns Christmas: her 1994 song 'All I Want for Christmas Is You' returns to number one every December, making her the first artist in history to top the charts in four separate decades with a single song.
She sang 'My Heart Will Go On' in a single take and reportedly disliked it, but it became the best-selling physical single by a female artist in history and the signature song of the 1990s.
She taught herself to play guitar as a child in Colombia and co-wrote most of her own material in two languages: her hips and her honesty both became internationally recognized cultural touchstones.
She has performed some of the most dangerous aerial acrobatics in pop concert history, including suspended harness routines in pouring rain, doing them live, without a safety net of lip-syncing.
She graduated high school at 16 as valedictorian while simultaneously recording her debut album, which debuted at number one and made her the best-selling new artist of 2001.
He is one of the few people to have achieved EGOT status, winning an Emmy, Grammy, Oscar, and Tony, and he completed the set at just 39 years old.
He grew up performing in a family band in Hawaii, impersonating Elvis at age four, and later became one of the last major pop artists to write, produce, and perform virtually all of his own material.
He was discovered at 13 via YouTube videos his mother posted, making him the first major pop star whose career was entirely launched by social media rather than a traditional record deal.

He ripped Janet Jackson's costume at the Super Bowl halftime show in 2004, an incident that ignited a national debate about media double standards; Jackson's career suffered while his continued to soar.
He slept on the London Underground and on friends' sofas for a year while trying to break through, before a chance connection with Jamie Foxx led to his first big break.
He auditioned as a solo act on The X Factor, was eliminated, then was placed into a group with four other rejected soloists, a decision that created one of the best-selling boy bands in history.

He built a following by posting six-second cover videos on Vine as a teenager, becoming one of the first musicians to convert a social media presence directly into a record deal and a number-one album.
He uploaded his first three mixtapes anonymously to YouTube with no press, no label, and no real name attached, amassing millions of streams before anyone knew who he was.
He was best known as a teenage wheelchair user on a Canadian high school drama before reinventing himself as a rapper and becoming one of the most-streamed artists in Spotify's history.
He was told by nearly every major label that a producer couldn't be a rapper, so he famously recorded vocals for 'Through the Wire' with his jaw wired shut after a near-fatal car accident.
His album To Pimp a Butterfly was the first rap album to win the Pulitzer Prize for Music, and his Super Bowl halftime show in 2025 became one of the most discussed in the event's history.
He was a white rapper from Detroit who was told he'd never make it in hip-hop, then released The Slim Shady LP and sold over 1.8 million copies in its first week.
He was arrested on murder charges the same week his debut album Doggystyle was released, the charges were eventually dropped, and the album went on to sell four million copies in its first year.
He produced both Snoop Dogg's and Eminem's debut albums, effectively launching two of the biggest careers in hip-hop history, then sold his Beats Electronics company to Apple for $3 billion.

He was shot nine times on his doorstep in 2000, survived, was dropped by his label, and used the story as the foundation of a mixtape buzz campaign that led to one of the best-selling debut albums of the decade.

He recorded so prolifically that new albums were released for years after his death, and his hologram appeared onstage at Coachella in 2012, reigniting debates about artistic legacy and whether he had faked his death.

He grew up selling crack on a Brooklyn street corner, taught himself to freestyle, and within a few years had crafted Ready to Die, considered one of the greatest debut albums in the history of hip-hop.
He released 'Tha Carter III' in 2008, which debuted at #1 and sold over a million copies in its first week, cementing him as the self-proclaimed 'Best Rapper Alive.'
His Astroworld album turned a Houston fairground nostalgia trip into a cultural phenomenon, complete with a rollercoaster named after him at the actual Six Flags.
He went from a Dallas teenager recording in his friend's closet to having 'Congratulations' rack up billions of streams before his debut album even dropped.
She became the first solo female rapper to win the Grammy for Best Rap Album, taking it home for 'Invasion of Privacy' in 2019.
She held the record for the most entries on the Billboard Hot 100 by a female artist for years, thanks in part to a seemingly endless string of guest verses.
Her 'Savage (Remix)' with Beyoncé hit #1 in 2020 even as she was finishing her degree in health administration at Texas Southern University.
As a founding member of N.W.A, he wrote most of the lyrics for 'Straight Outta Compton,' the album that scared the FBI enough to send the group an official warning letter.
He released 'Rock the Bells' at 16 and became one of the first rappers to prove the genre could sustain a decades-long career; his initials stand for 'Ladies Love Cool James.'
He and his Neptunes partner Chad Hugo won Producer of the Year at the Grammys twice, and his song 'Happy' from Despicable Me 2 became one of the best-selling singles of all time.
His 2004 album 'Confessions' spent 11 weeks at #1 and sold over 20 million copies, and he discovered and mentored Justin Bieber as a teenager.
At 16 he became the first male artist since Stevie Wonder to debut at #1 with a debut single, and he is widely considered one of the most technically skilled dancers in pop history.
He released 'Channel Orange' after a Tumblr post quietly disclosing a past relationship with a man, a rare, understated coming-out that reshaped conversations about queerness in R&B.
The Puerto Rican trap star became the most-streamed artist on Spotify globally three years in a row (2020-2022), the first non-English-language act to achieve that.
Her song 'Say So' went viral via TikTok before being officially released, a textbook case of internet-era music stardom that she followed with a Nicki Minaj remix that sent it to #1.
Her breakout video 'Video Games', shot on a flip camera and self-uploaded to YouTube in 2011, went viral and landed her a major-label deal almost overnight.
His 1975 album 'Born to Run' earned him simultaneous covers of Time and Newsweek, and his legendary live shows often run four hours, making him synonymous with rock as communal endurance.
As the Rolling Stones' frontman since 1962, he has performed across six decades and reportedly turned down a knighthood once before eventually accepting Sir Mick status in 2003.
He wrote 'Satisfaction' in his sleep, literally woke up, recorded the riff, and fell back asleep, and his lifestyle inspired Johnny Depp's portrayal of Captain Jack Sparrow.
Despite being the butt of 'worst Beatle' jokes, he was voted the greatest rock drummer of all time by Rolling Stone readers in 2011, with legends like Dave Grohl citing his feel.
Often called 'the quiet Beatle,' he wrote 'Something,' which Frank Sinatra called the greatest love song of 50 years, and organized the first major rock benefit concert in 1971.
1960s London fans spray-painted 'Clapton is God' on Underground walls, and he is the only three-time inductee into the Rock and Roll Hall of Fame: solo artist, Yardbird, and Cream member.
He played the final concert ever held at Shea Stadium in 2008, closing with 'Piano Man', a song he wrote while playing piano under a pseudonym in an LA bar when his career had stalled.
He earned the nickname 'the Godfather of Grunge' for his distorted guitar influence on a generation, and famously pulled his entire catalog from Spotify over a COVID misinformation dispute.
Her 1971 album 'Blue' is frequently ranked the greatest album ever made, and she invented over 50 open guitar tunings because childhood polio left her hand unable to form standard chord shapes.
She has had number-one hits in six consecutive decades, a record no other solo artist holds, from 'I Got You Babe' in the 1960s to 'Believe' in the 1990s, the latter pioneering the Auto-Tune effect.
As lead of the Supremes she scored 12 number-one hits, more than any 1960s act except the Beatles, and was later nominated for an Oscar for playing Billie Holiday.
He fused gospel, blues, and country so controversially that Georgia initially banned him, before later naming 'Georgia on My Mind' the official state song.
He performed at Folsom Prison in 1968 in a concert that revived his flagging career, and dressed entirely in black as a protest against poverty, turning a wardrobe choice into a political statement.
He wrote 'Crazy', one of the most recorded songs in history, and sold it to Patsy Cline when his own unconventional vocal style couldn't get radio play.

She secretly funded the Moderna COVID vaccine research and declined a state statue of herself, saying she hadn't earned it: while her song 'I Will Always Love You' was written as a business breakup letter to her mentor.

He became the best-selling solo artist in U.S. history, outselling Elvis Presley, and his alter-ego 'Chris Gaines', a fictional rock star invented for a film project, remains one of pop culture's strangest career detours.
'Come On Over' is the best-selling country album of all time and the best-selling album by any female artist in any genre, with over 40 million copies sold.
She won American Idol in 2005 and became its commercially most successful contestant, and her debut single 'Before He Cheats' spent 28 weeks at number one: the longest run by a debut country single.

He racked up more number-one singles on the Billboard Country Airplay chart than any other artist in the 2010s, and took over guardianship of his nephew and niece after losing both his brother and sister-in-law.
She survived the worst disaster in country music history when seven band members died in a 1991 plane crash, returned to touring within months, and later starred in a sitcom that ran for six seasons.
Her 'Breathe' album sold over 8 million copies and made her one of the few country artists to cross over to mainstream pop in the late 1990s.
His 'Live Like You Were Dying' won the Grammy for Best Country Song in 2005, and he proposed to Faith Hill while on tour together in 1996.
His double album 'Dangerous' spent a record 10 consecutive weeks at number one on the Billboard 200, the longest streak for any country album.
She played James Madison's 200-year-old crystal flute, borrowed from the Library of Congress, during a sold-out Washington D.C. show, the first time the instrument had been played in over a century.
She was 16 when 'Royals' became the first solo female artist born in the 1990s to top the Billboard Hot 100, a wry critique of hip-hop wealth fantasies from a suburban Auckland teenager.
She self-released music on SoundCloud as a teenager and built a fanbase before she had a record deal, eventually headlining arenas and releasing albums that spanned every genre.
She hid her face behind a giant bow-and-wig costume for years, and her 'Chandelier' music video featuring dancer Maddie Ziegler has over a billion YouTube views.
Her album 'Back to Black' won five Grammy Awards in 2008, including Record, Song, and Album of the Year, a record for a British female artist that stood for years.
He conceived 'The Wall' as a metaphor for his own emotional alienation, and the 1980-81 tour featured an actual 40-foot wall built across the stage mid-show.
He was fired from Black Sabbath and proceeded to have a bigger solo career, and became tabloid legend when he bit the head off a bat thrown onstage at a 1982 concert.
Guns N' Roses' 'Appetite for Destruction' is the best-selling debut album in U.S. history, with over 30 million copies sold worldwide.

His top-hat-and-cigarette silhouette is one of rock's most recognizable icons, and the opening riff of 'Sweet Child O' Mine', written in under a minute as a warmup exercise, became one of the most-played guitar riffs ever.
He negotiated directly with world leaders as part of his ONE Campaign, securing billions in aid for Africa, and U2's 'The Joshua Tree' made the Irish band the biggest rock act in the world in 1987.

After leaving The Police at their commercial peak, he spent years advocating for Amazon rainforest preservation and his claim of tantric discipline became a cultural punchline that somehow only increased his mystique.

He played Live Aid twice in 1985, once in London and once in Philadelphia the same day, after flying across the Atlantic on Concorde, and his 'In the Air Tonight' drum fill is one of the most air-drummed moments in pop history.

He wrote, produced, and played nearly every instrument on his debut album alone in a New York apartment, and won four consecutive Grammys for Best Male Rock Vocal Performance, a record that still stands.
His Woodstock performance in 1969 is one of rock history's most celebrated sets, and thirty years later his 'Supernatural' album won nine Grammys in a single night.

He produced Michael Jackson's 'Thriller,' 'Off the Wall,' and 'Bad', three of the best-selling albums ever, and with 28 Grammy Awards is among the most decorated individuals in the ceremony's history.
His 1990 performance of 'Nessun Dorma' at the BBC's World Cup broadcast introduced opera to hundreds of millions of TV viewers, making the aria a sporting-event staple.
Blind since the age of 12, he has sold over 90 million records, more than any classical or operatic artist in history, with a crossover career that kept him atop both classical and pop charts.

He founded the Silk Road Ensemble blending Central Asian, Chinese, and Western musical traditions, and played at Barack Obama's inauguration in 2009 in temperatures so cold the performance had to be pre-recorded.
He conceived 'Hamilton' while reading a biography on vacation and performed the first song at the White House for President Obama before the show won 11 Tonys.
He composed 'The Phantom of the Opera,' the longest-running show in Broadway history, which ran for 35 years, and co-wrote 'Jesus Christ Superstar' with Tim Rice before age 25.
He scored 'The Lion King,' 'Gladiator,' 'Inception,' and 'Interstellar,' pioneering non-Western instruments and electronic synthesis in blockbuster film scores.
He composed the themes for 'Star Wars,' 'Indiana Jones,' 'Jaws,' and 'Harry Potter', a run so ubiquitous that a single note can identify each film, and holds 54 Academy Award nominations.
He composed his Ninth Symphony, including the 'Ode to Joy', after he was completely deaf, and famously turned around at the premiere to see the standing ovation he couldn't hear.
He composed his first symphony at age 8 and over 600 works before dying at 35, and his 'Don Giovanni' overture was reportedly handed to musicians as the audience was still taking their seats.
He fathered 20 children and still produced over 1,000 compositions, but his music was largely forgotten after his death until Felix Mendelssohn revived the 'St. Matthew Passion' in 1829.
His ballets; 'Swan Lake,' 'The Nutcracker,' and 'Sleeping Beauty': defined classical ballet for Western audiences, and his 1812 Overture famously calls for real cannon fire.

He wrote over 500 concertos and taught at a Venetian orphanage whose all-girl orchestra became one of the most celebrated in Europe.
He scored 81 points in a single NBA game in 2006, the second-highest individual scoring game in history, and won an Academy Award for Best Animated Short Film in 2018.

His combination of 7-foot-1 height and 325 pounds made him arguably the most physically dominant player the NBA has ever seen, and he also had a platinum-selling rap single while still an active player.

His 6-foot-9 point guard frame broke every assumption about the position, and his 1991 announcement that he had contracted HIV, at a time of enormous stigma, changed public understanding of the disease.

His skyhook, an unstoppable one-handed shot no defense ever solved, remained effective across a 20-year career, and he held the NBA's all-time scoring record from 1984 until LeBron James surpassed it.
He revolutionized basketball by making the three-pointer the central offensive weapon, and his 2016 MVP was the first unanimous vote in NBA history.

His 7-foot frame with a shooting guard's skill set made him a position-less player before the term was common, and his decision to join the Golden State Warriors sparked one of the most polarizing debates in modern sports.
He went from selling trinkets on the streets of Athens as a teenager to winning two NBA MVP awards and an NBA championship before turning 27, earning the nickname 'The Greek Freak.'
He was the first player under 6-foot-1 to win an NBA MVP award, and his crossover dribble, cornrow braids, and tattoos made him the avatar of a generation's basketball identity.
Listed at 6-foot-4, he dominated players much taller through sheer physicality, and his Nike ad declaring 'I am not a role model' became one of sports history's most debated statements.
He led the league in rebounding seven consecutive seasons and won five championships, while also making solo diplomatic trips to North Korea and developing a personal friendship with Kim Jong-un.

She became the all-time leading scorer in NCAA Division I basketball history, men's or women's, breaking Pete Maravich's 53-year-old record, and her move to the WNBA shattered the league's viewership records.
He was the 199th pick in the 2000 NFL Draft, taken after six other quarterbacks, and went on to win seven Super Bowl rings, more than any franchise in history.

He was the first player to win Super Bowls with two different franchises as starting quarterback, and his forehead-slapping, audible-calling offense at the line of scrimmage changed how the entire position operates.
By age 28 he had already won four Super Bowl MVP awards, and his sidearm and no-look passes have expanded the vocabulary of what a quarterback throw can look like.
His Hail Mary to beat Dallas in 2016, thrown off his back foot as he was being tackled, is one of the most replayed moments in NFL playoff history.

He never threw an interception in four Super Bowl appearances, going 4-0, and his final-drive against Cincinnati with 3:10 left and no timeouts is still considered the greatest clutch performance in NFL history.
He holds virtually every significant NFL receiving record by margins considered unbreakable, and ran routes so precisely that defensive backs said they could set a watch by his cuts.

He started 297 consecutive regular-season games, a record for any position player, and his gunslinger style produced three consecutive MVP awards alongside some of the most memorable interceptions ever thrown.
He is the most productive tight end in NFL postseason history, and his relationship with Taylor Swift in 2023 turned his already high profile into a cross-cultural phenomenon.
He was the first running back to rush for 2,000 yards in a single NFL season (1973), and his 1995 murder trial became one of the most watched televised events in American history.

He scored from the halfway line on the Premier League's opening day of 1996, a 57-yard lob that became one of football's most iconic goals, and was the first British player to win league titles in four different countries.

His volley in the 2002 Champions League Final is consistently voted the greatest goal in the competition's history, and he led France to both the World Cup and European Championship in back-to-back years.
He scored two curling free-kicks against Real Madrid and received a standing ovation from the opposing Bernabeu crowd: one of the rarest moments in football history.

He scored twice in the 2002 World Cup Final after two years barely playing due to mysterious seizures, and his dominance was so total that 'Ronaldo' had to become 'CR7' just to distinguish a later player.
He became the world's most expensive footballer at 222 million euros in 2017, and was the fulcrum of the MSN trio with Messi and Suarez at Barcelona that won the treble in 2015.
He became only the second teenager in history, after Pelé, to score in a World Cup Final, doing so in France's 2018 triumph at age 19.
He scored 32 Premier League goals in 2017-18, breaking the record for a 38-game season, and has been notably vocal about being a Muslim in European football.

She scored two goals in the 2019 Women's World Cup Final and won both the Golden Boot and Golden Ball in the same tournament, and was the first white athlete and first woman to kneel during the national anthem.

She was the world's all-time leading goal scorer in international soccer, men's or women's, at the time of her retirement, and was the first female footballer to become a mainstream American sports icon.
He won the French Open 14 times, a number so surreal that the main court at Roland Garros was renamed in his honor while he was still winning on it.

She was the first Black woman ranked number one in the Open Era, and her campaign alongside her family to force equal prize money at Wimbledon succeeded in 2007.

She won four Major titles before turning 24, and her 2021 French Open withdrawal, disclosing depression and anxiety, ignited a global conversation about athlete mental health.
He became the youngest player in history to reach the ATP world number one ranking at 19, and his game draws comparisons to Federer, Nadal, and Djokovic simultaneously.
He won 18 Major championships, a record considered the sport's ultimate benchmark, and designed over 300 golf courses worldwide after retiring.
He transformed golf from a country-club pursuit into a televised mass sport in the 1960s, and the drink named after him, half iced tea, half lemonade, appears on menus worldwide.
He won four Major championships before turning 26, and has been the central figure in the PGA Tour vs. LIV Golf conflict, choosing to stay with the established tour when many peers defected.

He was selected first overall in 2005: the year the NHL cancelled its entire season, known as the 'Crosby Sweepstakes', and his overtime goal in the 2010 Olympics gold medal game is the most-watched moment in Canadian sports TV history.
He broke Wayne Gretzky's all-time NHL goal-scoring record in 2023, a mark that had stood for 30 years, and has led the league in goals scored nine times.

He scored a goal in five different ways in a single game in 1988, a feat never replicated, and later bought the Pittsburgh Penguins to save them from bankruptcy, then came out of retirement to play for the team he owned.
He made 'The Flip', a no-look shovel pass to nail Jeremy Giambi at the plate in the 2001 ALDS: one of the most instinctive defensive plays in playoff history, and his 3,000th hit was a home run.
He hit a home run in 1953 measured at 565 feet, coining the term 'tape-measure home run', and is considered the greatest what-if player in baseball history due to chronic knee injuries.

He held baseball's all-time home run record for 33 years, breaking Babe Ruth's mark in 1974 while receiving death threats, and preserved the racist hate mail he received to donate to the Baseball Hall of Fame.

He hit safely in 56 consecutive games in 1941, a record statisticians rank among the most improbable achievements in all of sports, and sent roses to Marilyn Monroe's grave three times a week for 20 years after her death.

His 2001 season of 73 home runs and a .515 on-base percentage is statistically the greatest offensive season in baseball history, and he was intentionally walked 120 times in 2004 as managers simply refused to pitch to him.

He is the only player in MLB history to be a legitimate ace starter and a legitimate cleanup hitter simultaneously, and in 2023 signed the largest contract in sports history, $700 million over 10 years, with the Los Angeles Dodgers.
Widely regarded as the best baseball player of his generation, he won three American League MVP awards by age 27 while playing his entire career for the Los Angeles Angels.
Retired with a perfect 50-0 professional record, his 2015 fight against Manny Pacquiao became the highest-grossing boxing match in history at the time.
The only boxer to win world titles in eight different weight divisions, he later served as a senator and presidential candidate in the Philippines.
He lost the 'Rumble in the Jungle' to Muhammad Ali in 1974, then made a stunning comeback to reclaim the heavyweight title at age 45, and later sold more than 100 million George Foreman Grills.
A four-time heavyweight champion best remembered for two brutal fights with Mike Tyson, the second of which ended when Tyson infamously bit off a chunk of his ear.

The most decorated Olympian of all time with 23 gold medals, he became a legend at the 2008 Beijing Games when he won eight gold medals in a single Olympics.
She has shattered her own world records in long-distance freestyle swimming multiple times, often winning races by margins that make her competitors look like they are in a different event.
Considered the greatest gymnast of all time, she has a signature vault named after her that is rated so dangerous the scoring code initially discouraged others from attempting it.

One of the most beloved American figure skaters ever, she won nine U.S. championships and five World titles but famously never captured the Olympic gold medal that eluded her twice.
Her career was derailed by the 1994 scandal in which her ex-husband arranged an attack on rival Nancy Kerrigan's knee before the U.S. Figure Skating Championships.

Known for his hugely popular horror game playthroughs, he raised millions for charity through livestreams and later co-directed an interactive YouTube film with a branching choose-your-own-adventure format.
The British YouTuber turned his rivalry with Logan Paul into two boxing matches that drew millions of pay-per-view buys and helped legitimize the YouTube-to-boxing pipeline.
She became TikTok's most-followed creator as a teenager almost purely through dance videos, reaching 100 million followers faster than anyone else on the platform.

Known for his theatrical villain persona complete with mullet wig and wraparound shades, he was mysteriously and permanently banned from Twitch in 2020 with no official explanation ever given.

The nephew of The Young Turks founder Cenk Uygur, he became Twitch's most-watched political streamer by blending leftist commentary with gaming and pop culture.

The MIT researcher turned podcaster is known for marathon multi-hour interviews with scientists, world leaders, and tech figures, as well as his signature black suit worn in every episode.

The former kickboxing world champion became one of the most-searched people on the internet in 2022 through viral misogynistic content, before being banned from major platforms and arrested in Romania.

His 12 Rules for Life sold millions of copies worldwide, and a viral 2018 Channel 4 interview in which he sparred with journalist Cathy Newman became one of the most-watched political interviews on YouTube.
She came out as gay in a 1997 episode of her sitcom in a landmark TV moment, later building a daytime talk show empire that ran for 19 seasons before ending amid workplace culture allegations.
Host of The Tonight Show known for his celebrity games and musical impressions, he became famous for making his guests laugh during SNL sketches, often inadvertently.
The host of Jimmy Kimmel Live is known for his annual 'I Told My Kids I Ate Their Halloween Candy' viral segments and his emotional monologues on healthcare and gun control.
He spent nine years playing an exaggerated right-wing pundit on The Colbert Report before dropping the character to take over The Late Show, where his Trump-era monologues went massively viral.

After a very public falling-out with NBC over The Tonight Show that became a national story, he reinvented late night by leaning into self-deprecating absurdism and eventually launched a hit travel podcast.
The host of Late Night and then the Late Show for over 30 years, he pioneered the modern talk show format and is famous for his gap-toothed wit and the Top Ten List.
He hosted The Tonight Show for 22 years and is as famous for his passion for collecting hundreds of vintage cars and motorcycles as he is for his massive chin.
He defined late-night television for 30 years as host of The Tonight Show, and his endorsement of a little-known stand-up on air was considered a career-making moment for comedians.
His satirical Daily Show became the primary source of news for millions of young Americans and is credited with influencing real political coverage during the Bush and Obama eras.
His long-form investigative comedy segments on Last Week Tonight have repeatedly caused real-world consequences, including crashing the FCC's website during his net neutrality piece.
The South African comedian took over The Daily Show from Jon Stewart and wrote a bestselling memoir, Born a Crime, about growing up mixed-race under apartheid.
He conducted over 50,000 interviews across his career, wearing his signature suspenders every night on CNN's Larry King Live for 25 years.
She was the first female co-anchor of a network evening news program and created The View, a daytime panel show that became a cultural institution for political and social debate.

The silver-haired CNN anchor is the son of Gloria Vanderbilt and became a household name for his on-the-ground hurricane and war coverage, including his emotional reporting from post-Katrina New Orleans.
Known as 'the most trusted man in America,' his on-air emotional reaction to JFK's assassination and his editorial declaring the Vietnam War a stalemate helped shape public opinion.
A former Nixon White House aide turned award-winning journalist, she conducted some of television's most-watched interviews, including a 2015 sit-down with Caitlyn Jenner.
She became the first female solo anchor of a major U.S. network evening news broadcast when she joined CBS News in 2006, after 15 years as a beloved Today show host.

The celebrity heart surgeon became a daytime TV health guru through Oprah's endorsement, later running for U.S. Senate in Pennsylvania where his promotion of miracle cures became a major liability.
Judge Judy Sheindlin's no-nonsense courtroom show became the longest-running court program in TV history, and she negotiated a salary that made her the highest-paid TV personality for years.

The seemingly omnipresent host ran American Idol and E! News simultaneously while also doing morning radio, building a multi-platform media empire before eventually taking over from Pat Sajak on Wheel of Fortune.

The self-proclaimed 'King of All Media' pushed the boundaries of broadcast radio for decades, earning record FCC fines, before moving to SiriusXM satellite radio in a $500 million deal.

The soft-spoken painter with the iconic afro taught millions to paint 'happy little trees' on his PBS show The Joy of Painting, filming over 400 episodes in 11 years and becoming a posthumous internet icon.

Fred Rogers testified before the U.S. Senate in 1969 in a quiet, sincere speech that convinced lawmakers to preserve $20 million in PBS funding, famously reciting the lyrics to 'What Do You Do with the Mad That You Feel.'

The exuberant Australian 'Crocodile Hunter' brought wildlife conservation to millions through his fearless hands-on encounters with dangerous animals, before dying tragically from a stingray barb in 2006.
The former New York chef turned the travel food show into literary television with Parts Unknown, earning Peabody Awards for episodes that used food as a lens into geopolitics and human stories.

He co-created and starred in Seinfeld, often called the greatest sitcom ever made, built around observational comedy about nothing, and famously turned down $5 million per episode to end the show on his own terms.
His 1996 special Bring the Pain is considered one of the greatest stand-up specials ever recorded, and his commentary on race in America made him one of comedy's sharpest social critics.
He famously walked away from a $50 million Comedy Central deal in 2005 at the height of Chappelle's Show's popularity, later returning with Netflix specials that reignited fierce cultural debate.
He sold out Madison Square Garden multiple times and became one of Hollywood's highest-paid actors, building a comedy-to-film crossover career rivaled by few stand-ups in history.

The British comedian pioneered a freewheeling, history-obsessed rambling style, often performing in heels and makeup, including a famous hour-long bit about the British Empire and Darth Vader's cafeteria.
He created the original British version of The Office, widely considered one of the greatest sitcoms ever made, and became known for his fearlessly provocative Golden Globes hosting appearances.

Her deliberately offensive and taboo-breaking comedy style made her one of the most polarizing voices in stand-up, and her 2005 mockumentary Jesus Is Magic blended music and comedy in an unusual format.
Her sketch series Inside Amy Schumer won a Peabody Award and she became one of comedy's biggest names after her film Trainwreck, co-written with Judd Apatow, was a surprise box-office hit.
His 'Seven Words You Can Never Say on Television' routine led to a landmark 1978 Supreme Court case that defined the FCC's power to regulate broadcast content.
His brutally autobiographical comedy about race, addiction, and his own near-fatal self-immolation set the template for confessional stand-up and influenced virtually every comedian who followed him.
His clean family-friendly comedy made him one of America's most beloved entertainers for decades, before he was convicted of sexual assault in 2018 following accusations from dozens of women.
The acid-tongued comedian broke glass ceilings as one of the first women to host a late-night talk show and reinvented herself multiple times, becoming famous for her red-carpet fashion commentary.
He entertained U.S. troops overseas for nearly 50 years from World War II through the Gulf War, and his 14 Academy Awards hosting stints remain a record.

His 'I don't get no respect' catchphrase and bulging-eyed delivery made him a comedy icon, but he didn't achieve mainstream fame until his mid-40s, making his comeback one of comedy's great late-bloomer stories.
He started as a teenage YouTube comedian and evolved into a writer-director whose Netflix special Inside, filmed entirely alone during COVID-19 lockdown, won three Emmy Awards.

His meticulous, storytelling-heavy stand-up style drew comparisons to classic comedians, but his personal life became tabloid fodder when he entered rehab and left his wife in a very public 2021 spiral.
The Saturday Night Live cast member became as famous for his high-profile celebrity relationships, including a brief engagement to Ariana Grande, as for his self-deprecating tattoo-covered persona.
One of the original supermodels and the first Black woman on the cover of French Vogue, she was also notorious for a series of phone-throwing incidents involving her personal assistants.
Her iconic mole and athletic physique made her one of the defining supermodels of the late 1980s and 1990s, and she appeared on over 500 magazine covers during her career.
She was the first Black woman to appear on the covers of the Sports Illustrated Swimsuit Issue and the Victoria's Secret catalog, later creating and hosting America's Next Top Model.
The German supermodel became a cultural fixture as the longtime host and producer of Project Runway and as a Victoria's Secret Angel, famous for her elaborate and theatrical Halloween costumes.

Her waif-like look in the early 1990s defined a controversial shift in fashion aesthetics and she famously appeared in Calvin Klein campaigns that became some of the most talked-about ads of the decade.
At her peak she was the world's highest-paid model for 15 consecutive years and walked the final Victoria's Secret Fashion Show in 2018 in one of fashion's most celebrated farewell moments.
Named 'Model of the Year' by industry bible Models.com multiple times, she wore the iconic spray-painted 'naked dress' at the 2016 Cannes Film Festival in one of fashion's most viral moments.
She became one of the most in-demand runway models of her generation while maintaining a massive social media presence, and walked in a record number of shows during a single Fashion Week season.

Transitioning from Keeping Up with the Kardashians to the highest-paid model in the world by 2018, she became the face of dozens of luxury brands, but a Pepsi ad appearance sparked one of the decade's biggest advertising controversies.
One of the most recognizable runway models of the 2010s, she leveraged her career into tech philanthropy by founding Kode with Klossy, a coding camp for young women.
Born Ralph Lifshitz in the Bronx, he built a global empire from a line of wide neckties and turned the polo shirt into an American status symbol, without ever having formally studied fashion design.
His minimalist aesthetic and provocative advertising, featuring a 15-year-old Brooke Shields saying 'nothing comes between me and my Calvins': made him as famous for controversy as for design.
He turned preppy American sportswear into a global brand by being one of the first designers to embrace hip-hop culture in the 1990s, a crossover that transformed the brand's identity.
She took over her brother Gianni's fashion house after his murder in 1997 and maintained its maximalist, rock-star glamour identity, becoming as recognizable a figure as the brand itself.
The Italian designer who made fashion theatrical and sexy was murdered outside his Miami mansion in 1997 by serial killer Andrew Cunanan, in one of fashion's most shocking tragedies.
He dressed Richard Gere in American Gigolo in 1980 and is credited almost single-handedly with changing how men dressed in suits, removing the structure and making tailoring soft and unlined.
The ponytailed, fan-carrying creative director of Chanel for over 35 years, he also simultaneously helmed Fendi and his own label while producing a legendary volume of sketches and opinions.
He is credited with inventing the modern women's power suit, the Le Smoking tuxedo, in 1966, a garment so radical that a woman was once turned away from a Paris restaurant for wearing it.

He was fired from Perry Ellis after his 1993 'grunge collection' was deemed a commercial disaster, only for that same collection to later be recognized as one of the most influential in American fashion history.
He revived Gucci from near-bankruptcy in the 1990s with overtly sexual, glamorous designs, then built his own namesake brand spanning fashion, beauty, and eventually film directing.
A figure skater who failed to make the 1968 U.S. Olympic team, she pivoted to fashion and transformed the bridal industry in her 40s, becoming the definitive name in high-end wedding gowns.

The icy, bob-haired editor-in-chief of Vogue has wielded more power over the fashion industry than perhaps any other individual for four decades, and inspired the character Miranda Priestly in The Devil Wears Prada.
A 2007 sex tape leak became the unlikely launchpad for a media empire spanning reality TV, a billion-dollar shapewear brand (SKIMS), and a serious push toward criminal justice reform advocacy.
The eldest Kardashian sister launched the lifestyle brand Poosh and made headlines when her lavish wedding to drummer Travis Barker in Italy became one of the most-covered celebrity events of 2022.
She built a fitness-focused brand and starred in Revenge Body after her very public relationship troubles played out on television, becoming the Kardashian most associated with personal reinvention.
The self-described 'momager' orchestrated the rise of the entire Kardashian-Jenner media empire, negotiating deals and producing Keeping Up with the Kardashians for 20 seasons.

She built Kylie Cosmetics largely through Instagram and Snapchat into a brand Forbes initially valued at nearly $1 billion, making her one of the youngest self-made billionaires, a label that later sparked significant controversy.
Won the 1976 Olympic decathlon as Bruce Jenner, widely celebrated as the world's greatest athlete, before later becoming a prominent transgender public figure.
Her leaked 2003 home video and the reality show 'The Simple Life' made her one of the first modern celebrities famous largely for being famous.
Holds three Michelin stars at his London restaurant and became a global TV phenomenon by screaming at amateur cooks on 'Hell's Kitchen' and 'MasterChef'.
A former OSS spy who didn't learn to cook until her 30s, she brought French cuisine to American kitchens through her landmark 1961 book 'Mastering the Art of French Cooking'.
Revolutionized upscale American dining in the 1980s with smoked-salmon pizza at Spago, and has catered the Governors Ball after the Oscars for decades.
A British chef who launched a real-world campaign to overhaul school lunch menus in the UK and US, taking on governments over children's nutrition.
Built his empire around bold Southwestern flavors and became famous for staging surprise 'throwdowns' against hometown cooks on his Food Network show.
Popularized the concept of the '30-Minute Meal' and built a media empire from a simple cooking segment, never having attended culinary school.
Bleached spiky hair, backward sunglasses, and a '67 Camaro are his trademarks as the self-styled 'Mayor of Flavortown' on 'Diners, Drive-Ins and Dives'.
Turkish butcher Nusret Gökçe became a global meme in 2017 for his theatrical slow-motion salt sprinkle over a perfectly rested steak.
Has published over 60 horror novels under his own name and the pseudonym Richard Bachman, with works like 'The Shining' and 'It' defining modern horror.
A single mother on welfare, she wrote the first Harry Potter novel in Edinburgh cafés and went on to sell over 500 million books worldwide.
Her 1969 memoir 'I Know Why the Caged Bird Sings' was one of the first autobiographies by a Black American woman to reach a mainstream audience, and she recited a poem at Bill Clinton's inauguration.
The first African American woman to win the Nobel Prize in Literature, known for 'Beloved', a ghost story rooted in the brutal reality of American slavery.
A Canadian writer whose 1985 dystopian novel 'The Handmaid's Tale' surged back onto bestseller lists and spawned a hit TV series after the 2016 US election.
His 1988 novel 'The Satanic Verses' led Iran's Ayatollah Khomeini to issue a fatwa calling for his death, forcing him into hiding for nearly a decade.
Blends mythology, folklore, and fantasy in works like 'American Gods' and 'Sandman', the groundbreaking DC Comics series that helped legitimize graphic novels as literature.
Created the brutal, sprawling fantasy world of Westeros in 'A Song of Ice and Fire', inspiring 'Game of Thrones' and making fans wait decades for the next book.
'The Da Vinci Code' sparked worldwide controversy in 2003 by weaving conspiracy theories about the Catholic Church and the Holy Grail into a thriller that sold 80 million copies.
Has had more New York Times #1 bestsellers than any other author in history and reportedly sells roughly one book every 23 minutes around the world.
A former Mississippi defense attorney who wrote his first legal thriller 'A Time to Kill' before dawn each morning and went on to define the legal thriller genre.
Popularized ideas like the '10,000-hour rule' and the 'tipping point' in accessible, anecdote-driven books that made social science feel like a page-turner.
A WWII RAF fighter pilot and spy turned beloved children's author, whose dark, subversive stories like 'Charlie and the Chocolate Factory' also hide a very adult edge.
Theodor Geisel wrote 'The Cat in the Hat' using a deliberately limited vocabulary of 236 words, on a bet that he couldn't write an engaging children's book with so few.
A lifelong atheist who converted to Christianity after late-night walks with J.R.R. Tolkien, and hid a complete Christian allegory inside the 'Chronicles of Narnia'.
Published only one novel in her lifetime, 'To Kill a Mockingbird', which won the Pulitzer Prize in 1961 and became one of the most assigned books in American schools.
The gilded parties, bootleg champagne, and doomed romance of 1920s Long Island he captured in 'The Great Gatsby' were drawn largely from his own turbulent life.
His Depression-era novel 'The Grapes of Wrath' about Dust Bowl migrants was so politically explosive that it was burned in several California counties.
'Fahrenheit 451', his 1953 novel about book-burning, was itself ironically censored in some school editions: passages about religion and alcohol were quietly removed.

Wrote or edited over 500 books spanning almost every major category in the Dewey Decimal System, and his 'Three Laws of Robotics' shaped how science fiction, and real AI ethics, thinks about machines.
His 1898 novel 'The War of the Worlds' was so convincingly adapted as a 1938 radio broadcast by Orson Welles that it allegedly caused widespread panic across the US.
His 19th-century adventure novels predicted the submarine, the moon rocket, and videoconferencing with uncanny accuracy, earning him the title 'Father of Science Fiction'.
Published 'The Catcher in the Rye' in 1951 and then retreated into total seclusion in rural New Hampshire, refusing interviews and publishing nothing more for decades.
A survivor of the Allied firebombing of Dresden, sheltering in a slaughterhouse, he turned that trauma into 'Slaughterhouse-Five', one of the great antiwar novels.
His 1966 'Black and White Ball' at the Plaza Hotel was dubbed the 'Party of the Century', but his unfinished novel 'Answered Prayers' destroyed his high-society friendships forever.
Invented 'Gonzo journalism' by embedding himself so deeply in stories, riding with the Hells Angels, covering Nixon's 1972 campaign, that he became the story.
Wrote 'Brave New World' in just four months in 1931; he and George Orwell both died on the same day, November 22, 1963, overshadowed by JFK's assassination.
Her semi-autobiographical novel 'The Bell Jar' and her raw, confessional poetry redefined how personal anguish could be turned into literary art before her death at 30.
Turned a Campbell's Soup can and Marilyn Monroe's face into high art, and his New York studio 'The Factory' became the epicenter of 1960s pop culture.
Pioneered 'action painting' by laying canvas on the floor and dripping, flinging, and pouring paint, a method Life magazine celebrated by asking 'Is he the greatest living painter in the US?'
Spent decades painting the bleached skulls and vast desert landscapes of New Mexico, becoming the 'Mother of American Modernism' while largely shunning the New York art world.
Spent 47 years illustrating the cover of the Saturday Evening Post, creating an idealized portrait of American small-town life that became inseparable from US nostalgia.
An anonymous British street artist whose satirical stencils appear overnight on walls from London to Gaza, and whose painting famously shredded itself moments after selling at auction for £1 million.
Rose from a New York graffiti writer tagging under the name 'SAMO©' to sell paintings for millions before dying of a heroin overdose at just 27.
Borrowed the bold outlines, Ben-Day dots, and melodrama of comic strips to create monumental fine-art paintings that blurred the line between popular culture and high art.
After illness confined him to a wheelchair in his 70s, he invented an entirely new medium, cutting and arranging painted paper, producing some of his most celebrated works.
Painted his famous water lily series while nearly blind from cataracts, and created an outdoor studio pond at Giverny that he tended as obsessively as a canvas.
Continued painting almost until his death despite crippling arthritis that left him unable to hold a brush, reportedly having it tied to his hand.
Obsessively painted and sculpted ballet dancers, producing over 600 works on the subject, despite never romanticizing the art form, showing the grueling labor behind the grace.
Worked in near isolation in Provence for decades, but his geometric approach to apples, mountains, and card players directly inspired Picasso and became the bridge to Cubism.
His 1893 painting 'The Scream', inspired by a real moment of existential dread on a walk, has become one of the most recognizable images of modern anxiety.
His golden, ornate portrait 'Woman in Gold' was looted by Nazis and became the subject of a landmark legal battle won by the subject's niece over 60 years later.
Submitted a factory-bought urinal titled 'Fountain' to an art exhibition in 1917, asking whether context alone makes something art, a question the art world still hasn't settled.
A Japanese artist who has voluntarily lived in a psychiatric institution since 1977, continuing to create from there; her mirrored 'Infinity Rooms' now draw hours-long queues worldwide.
His sweeping black-and-white photographs of Yosemite and the American West, often made on large-format cameras on treacherous terrain, helped secure the expansion of the national park system.
Her Rolling Stone cover images, including the last photo of John Lennon hours before his murder, and her intimate Vanity Fair celebrity portraits defined decades of American pop culture photography.
Led the first expedition to circumnavigate the globe, though he was killed in the Philippines before completing the voyage, and only 18 of his original 270 crew members made it home.
Led the Corps of Discovery 8,000 miles across unmapped North America to the Pacific Ocean between 1804 and 1806, guided in large part by a teenage Shoshone woman.
Beat Robert Falcon Scott to the South Pole in December 1911 by using sled dogs efficiently and preparing obsessively, while Scott's rival British team perished on the return journey.
When his ship 'Endurance' was crushed by Antarctic ice, he led all 27 of his crew to safety across hundreds of miles of open ocean in a lifeboat, losing not a single man.
On May 29, 1953, the New Zealand beekeeper became one of the first two confirmed humans to summit Everest; he learned of his knighthood while still on the mountain.
The Sherpa mountaineer who summited Everest alongside Edmund Hillary declined to say who stepped onto the top first, calling it a private moment shared between friends.
Co-invented the Aqua-Lung scuba device in 1943 and spent decades aboard his ship Calypso filming the underwater world, bringing the ocean into living rooms worldwide.
His failed 1974 attempt to rocket across Snake River Canyon attracted more live TV viewers than the moon landing, despite ending in an inglorious splashdown.
In 2012 he skydived from a capsule 24 miles above Earth, breaking the sound barrier in freefall and setting multiple world records before landing safely in the New Mexico desert.
Commanded the fastest armored advance in military history across France in 1944, and controversially slapped a shell-shocked soldier in a military hospital, nearly ending his career.
Famously pledged 'I shall return' when forced out of the Philippines in 1942, waded ashore to fulfill the promise in 1944, and later accepted Japan's formal surrender on the USS Missouri.
Turned down command of the Union Army to instead lead the Confederate Army of Northern Virginia, believing his loyalty to Virginia outweighed his duty to the United States.
Earned his nickname by standing firm 'like a stone wall' at the First Battle of Bull Run; he was accidentally shot by his own troops at Chancellorsville and died days later.
His 'March to the Sea' through Georgia in 1864 deliberately destroyed civilian infrastructure to break the South's will to fight, pioneering the concept of total war.
So respected by both sides in North Africa that Churchill praised him in Parliament, and so feared that Allied commanders pinned his portrait on their walls as a motivational reminder.
Ran Chicago's criminal empire during Prohibition but was ultimately brought down not by violence charges, but by a federal tax evasion conviction in 1931.
Known as the 'Teflon Don' after three high-profile acquittals, he was finally convicted in 1992 largely because his underboss Sammy 'The Bull' Gravano turned government witness.
At his peak the Colombian cocaine kingpin was smuggling 15 tons of cocaine into the US per day and reportedly spending $2,500 a month just on rubber bands for his cash.
The Depression-era outlaw couple robbed banks and small stores across five states, becoming folk anti-heroes before being ambushed in a hail of 130 bullets on a Louisiana road in 1934.
Escaped from an 'escape-proof' Indiana jail using a wooden gun he'd whittled himself, before being betrayed by the 'Lady in Red' and shot outside a Chicago theater in 1934.
A former Confederate guerrilla who became America's most romanticized outlaw, he was shot in the back of the head by a fellow gang member hoping to collect a reward.
The New Mexico outlaw reportedly killed 21 men by his 21st birthday, historians put the real number closer to 8, and was shot dead by Sheriff Pat Garrett in a darkened room.
Charming and articulate enough to act as his own attorney, he confessed to 30 murders across seven states shortly before his 1989 execution, to the end fascinated by his own case.
Never personally committed the 1969 Tate-LaBianca murders himself but orchestrated them through his 'Family' cult, believing the killings would trigger a race war he called 'Helter Skelter'.
A Milwaukee factory worker who murdered and dismembered 17 men and boys between 1978 and 1991; one victim escaped and led police to the apartment where his crimes were finally uncovered.

A wandering Chinese scholar who spent 13 years traveling from court to court trying unsuccessfully to get rulers to adopt his ideas; his teachings became the foundation of Chinese society only after his death.
The ancient Chinese general's 'The Art of War', written around 500 BC, is still required reading at military academies and business schools worldwide.
Born a sheltered Hindu prince in Nepal, Siddhartha Gautama abandoned his palace and family at 29 to seek enlightenment, and attained it meditating beneath a fig tree.
A carpenter's son from Galilee whose three-year public ministry, death by crucifixion, and reported resurrection became the founding events of the world's largest religion.
The Prophet of Islam received the first Quranic revelations at age 40 in a cave outside Mecca, and within 23 years had united the warring tribes of Arabia under a new faith.
According to the Bible, he parted the Red Sea to lead the Israelites out of Egyptian slavery, received the Ten Commandments on Mount Sinai, and yet never entered the Promised Land himself.
Never wrote anything down; everything we know about him comes through his students, and his method of relentless questioning so irritated Athens that he was tried and forced to drink hemlock.
United most of Western Europe for the first time since Rome, was crowned Holy Roman Emperor on Christmas Day 800 AD, and is considered the founding father of both France and Germany.
The legendary British king whose court at Camelot, the Knights of the Round Table, and the quest for the Holy Grail have inspired literature and film for over a thousand years.
The 15th-century Wallachian prince who impaled tens of thousands of enemies on stakes was likely the historical inspiration for Bram Stoker's Count Dracula.
Broke with the Catholic Church and established the Church of England primarily so he could divorce his first wife, eventually having six wives in total: two of whom he beheaded.
Ruled France for 72 years, the longest reign of any major European monarch, and reportedly declared 'L'état, c'est moi' ('I am the state'), centralizing all power in himself.
The boy pharaoh died at around 19 and was largely forgotten for 3,000 years until Howard Carter opened his nearly intact tomb in 1922, triggering a worldwide obsession with ancient Egypt.
Ruled Egypt for 66 years, built more monuments and fathered more children (over 100) than any other pharaoh, and had his name carved so deeply into existing temples that successors couldn't erase it.
The Egyptian queen whose 1912 limestone bust is one of the most copied artworks of antiquity; she may have briefly ruled Egypt as pharaoh in her own right after Akhenaten's death.
The Babylonian king who inscribed one of the world's first written legal codes on a seven-foot stone column around 1754 BC, including the famous 'eye for an eye' principle.
With just 500 men, horses, and steel weapons, he toppled the Aztec Empire: aided decisively by smallpox and by alliances with peoples who resented Aztec rule.
An illiterate Spanish pig farmer who led the conquest of the Inca Empire, capturing Emperor Atahualpa in 1532 and demanding a ransom of gold that filled an entire room.
A Tennessee frontiersman and congressman who became a legend through his own tall tales, dying at the Alamo in 1836 and becoming the most mythologized figure of the American frontier.
Blazed the Wilderness Road through the Cumberland Gap, founding Boonesborough in Kentucky, and was captured twice by Shawnee warriors: one time being adopted into the tribe.
The Hunkpapa Lakota chief who united the Sioux and Cheyenne nations at the Battle of Little Bighorn in 1876, where they annihilated Custer's Seventh Cavalry.

Never allowed his photograph to be taken and refused to live on a reservation; killed in a disputed incident at a US Army fort in 1877, he is now the subject of the world's largest mountain sculpture.
The Apache leader who outmaneuvered thousands of US and Mexican troops for decades before surrendering in 1886; his name became a battle cry for American paratroopers in WWII.
The Powhatan chief's daughter who helped broker an uneasy peace between her people and Jamestown's English settlers, was later captured, converted to Christianity, and died in England aged around 21.
The Shoshone teenager who joined the Lewis and Clark expedition as an interpreter while carrying a newborn, and whose presence signaled to other tribes that the expedition was peaceful.

Left deaf and blind by illness at 19 months, she learned to read, write, and speak through the extraordinary teaching of Anne Sullivan, and went on to become a published author and political activist.

The quintessential Hollywood cowboy who starred in over 170 films, won an Oscar for 'True Grit', and became such a symbol of American masculinity that the US Congress awarded him the Congressional Gold Medal.
Choreographed and performed the legendary 'Singin' in the Rain' sequence in one take while running a high fever, redefining what dance could look like on film.
His partnership with Ginger Rogers in 1930s Hollywood musicals was so perfectly matched that it was said she did everything he did but backwards and in high heels.
Won the Academy Award for Best Actress for 'Kitty Foyle', a dramatic role, proving she was far more than Fred Astaire's dancing partner, though their ten films together remain iconic.
Won two Oscars for acting, launched a fitness video empire in the 1980s, and earned the lasting nickname 'Hanoi Jane' for a controversial visit to North Vietnam during the war.
The WWE's legendary 'Dead Man' character, played by Mark Calaway, went undefeated at WrestleMania for 21 years, a streak so iconic that its ending in 2014 left fans visibly in shock.
Stood 7'4" and weighed over 500 pounds, went undefeated in wrestling for 15 years, could reportedly drink 100 beers in a single sitting, and was immortalized in the 'OBEY' street art poster.

'To be the man, you gotta beat the man': the 'Nature Boy' won a record 16 world heavyweight championships and his sequined robes, 'WOO!' catchphrase, and figure-four leglock defined professional wrestling for four decades.
